response: The EU’s General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), in force since May 2018, applies to any organization processing the personal data of EU residents—regardless of where it is based—and grants rights to access, erasure, portability and requires explicit consent, appointment of Data Protection Officers for large‐scale processing, and breach notification within 72 hours. Non-compliance can trigger fines of up to 4 percent of global annual revenue or €20 million (whichever is higher); notable penalties include Meta’s €1.2 billion fine in 2023, Amazon’s €746 million sanction for ad targeting, and over €200 million in fines against Google. GDPR has served as the model for similar statutes worldwide, such as California’s CCPA, Brazil’s LGPD and India’s Digital Personal Data Protection Act. Critics contend that its complex requirements burden small businesses, stifle innovation, lead to ubiquitous—and annoying—cookie banners, and suffer from inconsistent enforcement, even as GDPR enshrines data privacy as a fundamental right and sets the global benchmark.
